title: Idaho Pro-Life State Senator Says Ending Abortion “Destroys the Pro-Life Movement”
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman exposes Idaho State Senator Fred Martin for telling a constituent that the Idaho Abortion Human Rights Act, a bill to abolish abortion, would "destroy the pro-life movement," and argues this reveals the financial and institutional incentives by which pro-life organizations like Right to Life of Idaho actively oppose abolition.
audiences: Pro-life voters reassessing their movement's leadership
Idaho constituents and activists tracking state legislators
Abolitionists building the case against pro-life incrementalism
Pastors and Christians weighing political alliances on abortion

questions.question: What did Idaho State Senator Fred Martin say would happen to the pro-life movement if abortion were ended in Idaho?
questions.audience: Idaho constituents and activists tracking state legislators
questions.answer: In a recording uploaded to Facebook, Martin told a constituent that passing the Idaho Abortion Human Rights Act, a bill to end abortion in the state, would destroy the pro-life movement.
questions.quote: A recording uploaded to Facebook on Sunday reveals pro-life Idaho State Senator Fred Martin telling a constituent that passing the Idaho Abortion Human Rights Act, a bill which would end abortion in the state, would “destroy the pro-life movement.”

questions.question: How does Silberman use the Emancipation Proclamation analogy to reframe Martin's complaint?
questions.audience: Abolitionists building the case against pro-life incrementalism
questions.answer: Silberman concedes Martin is correct that abolition would destroy the pro-life movement, but argues that is precisely the point: just as the Emancipation Proclamation rightly ended the anti-slavery movement by achieving its objective, abolishing abortion should end the pro-life movement.
questions.quote: Martin is correct. In the same way, the Emancipation Proclamation destroyed the anti-slavery movement. That was a good thing. Their objective had been achieved. So why does Martin speak of ending the pro-life movement like it’s a bad thing?

questions.question: What financial motive does the constituent suggest is behind pro-life organizations opposing the abolition bill?
questions.audience: Pro-life voters reassessing their movement's leadership
questions.answer: The constituent told Martin that Right to Life of Idaho and Idaho Chooses Life would lose their income and effectively be out of work if abortion were abolished, suggesting an institutional incentive to keep the pro-life movement in business rather than ending abortion.
questions.quote: “They would totally cease their income if [abortion were abolished]. You know that as well as I do. They’re out of work.”

questions.question: Is the opposition of Right to Life affiliates to abolition bills unique to Idaho?
questions.answer: No. Silberman points to a broader pattern of Right to Life chapters opposing abolition across the country, including spreading misinformation about abolition bills in Oklahoma and Indiana.
questions.quote: We can’t know someone’s motives for certain, but there is a pattern of Right to Life chapters opposing the abolition of abortion all around the country, in some cases even spreading misinformation about abolition bills in [Oklahoma](https://freethestates.org/2019/02/the-three-pro-life-men-keeping-abortion-legal-in-oklahoma/index.html) and Indiana to do it.

questions.question: What does Silberman make of Martin's stammered "If I could end abortions" remark?
questions.audience: Abolitionists building the case against pro-life incrementalism
questions.answer: Silberman reads it as Martin realizing in the moment that he already has the power to help end abortion by supporting the Abortion Human Rights Act, and pivoting to talk about his wife rather than admit he won't use the power he has.
questions.quote: At one point in the video, Martin says, “If I could end abortions, uh, I’d, uh,” at which point he changes the subject to how pro-life his wife is. This was likely Martin realizing that he can help to end abortion by supporting the Abortion Human Rights Act, meaning that he wouldn’t actually help abolish abortion if he could because he has the ability to do so right now and isn’t.

questions.question: How did Martin respond on Facebook when constituents confronted him about opposing the Abortion Human Rights Act?
questions.audience: Idaho constituents and activists tracking state legislators
questions.answer: Martin claimed not to know which bill the constituent was talking about and demanded a bill number, while citing his record of voting for or sponsoring every pro-life bill that had passed the Idaho Senate in the last seven years.
questions.quote: “What bill? What bill (by the bill number), so I can look it up to see what bill you are talking about. I have voted for or been the floor sponsor or bill of EVERY Pro-Life bill that has passed the Senate in the last 7 years.”

questions.question: Why does Silberman find Martin's "what bill?" response implausible?
questions.answer: The Facebook post he was responding to was full of references to the Abortion Human Rights Act and the recording, so Martin was either feigning ignorance or taunting constituents over the fact that Republican leaders had denied the bill a number and a hearing.
questions.quote: The Facebook post was full of references to the Abortion Human Rights Act and the recording, indicating that Martin was likely either hoping the constituents were unintelligent enough to believe that he didn’t know what they were talking about or he was taunting them with the fact that the bill had been denied a number and hearing by state Republican leaders.

questions.question: Who does Silberman identify as the actual obstacle to abolishing abortion in red states like Idaho?
questions.audience: Pastors and Christians weighing political alliances on abortion
questions.answer: He argues that in red states where Planned Parenthood and NARAL lack power, it is Republican leaders and National Right to Life's state-level affiliates who are keeping abortion legal.
questions.quote: Just like in Oklahoma and Indiana, it’s Republican leaders and National Right to Life’s state-level affiliates who are preventing abortion from being abolished. In red states like these where Planned Parenthood and NARAL are powerless to keep abortion legal, pro-life leaders are keeping it legal for them.

questions.question: Who authored the Idaho Abortion Human Rights Act referenced in the article?
questions.audience: Idaho constituents and activists tracking state legislators
questions.answer: The bill was authored by Idaho State Representatives Heather Scott of Blanchard and John Green of Post Falls, both Republicans.
questions.quote: Listen to the recording and watch a video explanation of the Abortion Human Rights Act, authored by State Reps. Heather Scott (R-Blanchard) and John Green (R-Post Falls), below.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Directly hyperlinked from this article as a parallel example of Right to Life affiliates spreading misinformation against an abolition bill.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho Sen. Brent Crane Insults and Intimidates Family, Promises to Keep Abortion Legal
related.reason: Another Idaho pro-life state senator caught on record working to keep abortion legal, mirroring Martin's behavior.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: Nullification Doesn't Happen in Reality
related.reason: Companion piece on Idaho pro-life legislators using rhetorical dodges to oppose abolition through state nullification.

related.title: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
related.reason: Concrete Indiana case study of pro-life leadership killing an abolition bill, supporting Silberman's claim of a national pattern.

related.title: Don't Be Impressed by Politicians' Claim of Being 100% Pro-Life
related.reason: Generalizes the lesson from Martin's Facebook defense that a long pro-life voting record can coexist with blocking actual abolition.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Provides the broader abolitionist framework for why pro-life institutions oppose bills that would actually end abortion.

related.title: My Pro-Life to Abolitionist Conversion
related.reason: First-person counterpart for readers persuaded by Silberman's critique who want to understand how pro-lifers move toward abolition.
